_comment: Per-chart narratives. CI (build_prompt.py -> GitHub Models -> write_narrative.py) overwrites these from live numbers; this committed copy is the technical fallback so the page always reads well. source = 'fallback' | 'github-models'.

notes.cape: The Shiller CAPE (Cyclically Adjusted Price-to-Earnings) ratio divides the current price of the S&P 500 by the average inflation-adjusted earnings over the past ten years, smoothing cyclicality to gauge valuation. As of July 2026, the CAPE stands at 41.94, far above its 140-year average of 17.3 and approaching the all-time peak of 44.19 recorded in December 1999. Elevated CAPE readings historically precede below-average forward returns; for example, levels above 39 have correlated with average -4% returns over one year and -20% over two years. However, CAPE timing is imprecise—high valuations can persist amid low interest rates or structural earnings changes, hence it signals valuation extremes but not the timing of market reversals.
notes.buffett: The Buffett Indicator, defined as total US equity market capitalization divided by GDP, reflects how stretched valuations are relative to economic output. At 231.7% in June 2026, it dramatically exceeds prior cycle peaks such as 146% at the 2000 dot-com bubble and 110% in 2007, positioning the market as historically expensive. Warren Buffett has warned that readings above 200% constitute "playing with fire," as market capitalization well ahead of economic fundamentals suggests vulnerability to reversion. This ratio captures systemic overvaluation risk but lacks predictive timing power, since liquidity conditions and investor sentiment can sustain elevated multiples for extended periods.
notes.concentration: Concentration measures the portfolio weight of a small number of stocks or sectors within the index, evidencing market breadth and diversification risk. Currently, the top 5 S&P 500 stocks constitute approximately 29.8%, while the tech sector alone accounts for 48%; the top 10's share has surged to 41.0% in 2026 from 17.5% in 2015. Heightened concentration amplifies downside risk because shocks to a few large names or sectors can disproportionately impact the whole market. Although concentration signals fragility, it does not indicate when stress will arrive, as investor preference for growth and technology has structurally increased these weights over the past decade.
notes.yieldcurve: The yield curve spread between 10-year Treasury yields and 3-month Treasury rates currently sits at 0.73 points (July 2026), indicating a positive slope but lower than historical long-term averages, with the New York Fed recession probability at 31%. The yield curve measures market expectations for economic growth and inflation; close-to-flat or inverted curves often precede recessions and equity downturns due to anticipated tightening or slowdown. This metric functions as a liquidity and economic risk barometer, informing credit conditions and investor risk appetite. However, yield curve signals can generate false positives or change with monetary policy regimes, requiring contextual interpretation alongside other indicators.
notes.drypowder: "Dry powder" refers to cash reserves or liquid assets held by investors ready for deployment in opportunities; Berkshire Hathaway's record Q1 2026 cash balance reached $397.4 billion. Despite this record, 79% of institutions surveyed expect a contraction in dry powder, suggesting potential tightening of liquidity availability. High dry powder denotes the capacity to support asset prices or capitalize on market dislocations, effectively sustaining the "music" in the market. Yet actual deployment depends on confidence and conditions, meaning dry powder levels alone do not schedule market turning points but indicate the potential amplitude of adjustment when liquidity shifts.
notes.finale: While elevated valuations such as high CAPE and Buffett ratios define the potential magnitude of market corrections—how severe losses could be—they do not predict timing. The "music stops" when liquidity conditions abruptly tighten, often through a triggering event that reverses investor sentiment or funding availability. One identified trigger window is December 2026 through May 2027, corresponding to IPO lock-up expirations for major issuers like SpaceX, OpenAI, and Anthropic; these expirations can increase supply of tradable shares and test liquidity. This confluence highlights that valuation metrics set vulnerability levels, but market rotations occur when a liquidity shock interrupts the ongoing momentum, ending the era of cheap money and forcing investors to find fewer chairs.
